explanation: Bypasses the rule by utilizing the Windows 8.3 short filename feature. Since the rule looks for the exact string 'gatherNetworkInfo.vbs' in the command line, using the short name 'GATHER~1.VBS' evades the selection_cli condition while still executing the same file.

Bypasses the rule by copying the built-in script to a new directory with an entirely different file name before executing it. The cscript.exe process will run, but the command line will contain 'network_check.vbs' instead of 'gatherNetworkInfo.vbs', evading detection.

The rule specifically looks for the string 'gatherNetworkInfo.vbs' in the command line. By copying the VBScript to a new file with a different name or extension and executing it from that new location, the command line argument no longer matches the 'selection_cli' filter.

In some SIEM environments, 'contains' filters might be bypassed by using specific script engine flags or unusual path formatting (like short 8.3 filenames, e.g., GATHER~1.VBS) that prevent the literal string match depending on how the logs are parsed and normalized.

Instead of calling cscript.exe or wscript.exe (which triggers 'selection_img'), the attacker can read the content of the VBScript and execute the logic through a different interpreter like PowerShell, or use an alternative host to execute the script code, bypassing both the image and command line requirements.
